glossary.def: A learned simulator of an environment's dynamics: given a compact latent summary of the present and a proposed action, it predicts the next state (and often the reward). Once trained, it can be rolled forward in imagination to generate futures without touching the real environment, supporting prediction, generation, and planning.

A transition function that predicts the next state in a low-dimensional learned latent space rather than in raw observation (pixel) space. Working in the latent keeps only what is needed to predict the future, so the model spends capacity on behaviorally relevant structure instead of photo-realistic but irrelevant detail.

A trajectory generated entirely inside a world model by feeding each predicted next state back into the transition function over a horizon, with no real environment steps. Dreamer trains an actor and critic on such rollouts; because a latent forward pass is far cheaper than a real step, this yields large sample-efficiency gains.

Dreamer's world model, which splits the latent into a deterministic recurrent hidden state that carries history and a stochastic state sampled from a learned distribution. It is trained variationally by pulling a prior (transition-only) toward a posterior (observation-conditioned) via a KL term, so the transition learns to anticipate latents before observations arrive.

LeCun's architecture that predicts in representation space rather than pixel space: it encodes both an input and a target into embeddings and trains a predictor to map the input embedding to the target embedding, with no decoder or reconstruction loss. By predicting an embedding it can discard unpredictable detail, but it requires stop-gradient and EMA targets to avoid representation collapse.

The degenerate failure where an encoder maps every input to the same constant embedding, driving a joint-embedding loss to zero while learning nothing useful. JEPA and similar methods prevent it with an EMA target encoder, stop-gradient, and variance/covariance regularizers.

Genie's mechanism for learning a controllable simulator from action-unlabelled video: for each pair of consecutive frames it infers a discrete latent action, from a tiny codebook, that best lets the dynamics model reconstruct the next frame. The small codebook forces the actions to capture controllable, recurring changes (jump, move) rather than memorize pixels.

Planning by rolling a learned model forward over a horizon, scoring candidate action sequences by their imagined discounted return plus a learned terminal value, executing only the first action of the best plan, then re-planning. MuZero is the canonical instance; the danger is that search amplifies model error, so plans can exploit dynamics the world does not actually have.

refs.cite: Ha, D. & Schmidhuber, J. (2018). World Models. NeurIPS 2018.
LeCun, Y. (2022). A Path Towards Autonomous Machine Intelligence (JEPA position paper).
Hafner, D., Pasukonis, J., Ba, J. & Lillicrap, T. (2023). Mastering Diverse Domains through World Models (DreamerV3).
Bruce, J. et al. (2024). Genie: Generative Interactive Environments. ICML 2024.
Schrittwieser, J. et al. (2020). Mastering Atari, Go, Chess and Shogi by Planning with a Learned Model (MuZero). Nature.
Valevski, D. et al. (2024). Diffusion Models Are Real-Time Game Engines (GameNGen).
